Upgrade v0.2.11 (#813)

* Make SafetyWindow a Parameter (#809)

# Make SafetyWindow a Parameter
## What problem does this solve?
During a recent chain halt, we skipped making SafetyWindow a parameter to reduce risk and expedite recovery. We fixed it at 50. The problem: 
- Most urgently, this broke our integration tests, because there was no way to override the value, breaking all CPoC Integration tests because Epochs are shorter than 50 blocks for testing, meaning no CPoC ever executes
- Secondarily, but also important, this puts an important value impacting chain execution under proper governance control.

## How do you know this is a real problem?
Currently there are at least 4 failing integration tests, obscuring our ability to verify other code problems.

## How does this solve the problem?
- Add a new parameter to EpochParameters for SafetyWindow (default to 50, per the current value)
- Remove the hard coded value
- Fix tests to override this to 0

## What risks does this introduce? How can we mitigate those risks?
- There is very little risk, but an upgrade issue would be caught via testnet update testing, done for every upgrade.

## How do you know this PR fixes the problem?
Integration Tests now pass when they failed.

* Unified Permissions (#760)

# Unified Permission handling
## What problem does this solve?
Permissions for sending messages have been a bit sloppy, yet they are crucial for network security. Some messages had no permissions, some had too little. Changing them/updating them was a bit error prone and done in different ways. There was significant code duplication in various auth verification as well.

## How do you know this is a real problem?
This was not only reported by Certik, it is manifestly obvious from the code that some messages have inadequate authorization.

## How does this solve the problem?
A single procedure for setting permissions. `permissions.go` now contains:
1. A list of permissions available (multiple permissions can be required)
2. A mapping of all messages to permissions
3. Implementation of each permission check

Each message now calls CheckPermission _first thing_, and rejects immediately if the Sender doesn't have permission to execute the message. The work on the allow lists for training was integrated into this as well.

CheckPermission ALSO takes the Permissions used. If they do not match the settings in permissions.go, the call will fail. The purpose of this is to have an easy way to see Permissions both in the aggregate (in permissions.go) and for each individual message (when reviewing, for instance).

This is actually protected only through a unit test checking that the permissions listed in permissions.go match what is passed in, rather than enforced at runtime. Runtime enforcement was found to have a measurable performance hit.

## What risks does this introduce? How can we mitigate those risks?
The biggest immediate risk is that a new permission will be too restrictive, or incorrect, and will cause issues on chain. 
- Testermint will likely catch any major issues that make this mistake, so Integration Tests will be especially important.
- Reviewing which messages have which permissions is now quite easy, as looking at permissions.go is guaranteed to show the entire story.

A secondary risk is that this will degrade over time, and new messages will not use the system, and we end up right where we left off.

This is addressed by the new unit tests that will fail if new messages are introduced and don't use CheckPermission

A final risk is this might hit perf of some of our high volume messages. I've mitigated this by carefully using `Has` instead of `Get` for the various checks, as well as creating the `ActiveParticipantsSet` collection to provide O(logN) lookup for ActiveParticipants (previously we could only check ActiveParticipants by serializing the ENTIRE structure and then iterating over each Participant). This design is very fast, and does zero serializing at all. Since we only set ActiveParticipants at the very beginning of an Epoch and never again (for crytpographic verification), this is safe.

We have measured the performance of the serialization of the ActiveParticipants and it is signficant, and could add more than 10% or more (as the size grows) to each call that uses it, justifying this optimization.

## How do you know this PR fixes the problem?
Many unit tests have been added for specifically to verify permissions. In addition, a test was added that verifies that all message handlers use `CheckPermission` first thing, and that each message in the proto file has a handler.

## Other notes
There are two messages that do not quite fit the pattern:
- InvalidateInference
- RevalidateInference
Both of these are authenticated by the Group policy for the Epoch group in which the voting took place. Shoehorning this into the `CheckPermission` would have been inelegant and forced, so instead they use the `NoPermissions` permission and then authorize later on.

* StartInference and FinishInference performance improvements (#812)

# Start/FinishInference performance improvements

### 1. What problem does this solve?
`MsgStartInference` and `MsgFinishInference` take 10-50ms (and sometimes upwards of 50ms) to execute in production, which severely restricts network throughput and threatens block time stability. The heavy processing is largely caused by:
*   Heavy on-chain querying and recording of developer stats.
*   Duplicate participant and inference state writes across the completion flow.
*   Heavy `Decimal.Ln` (logarithmic) operations inside participant status (`ComputeStatus`) calculations.
*   Expensive epoch-group reads and `InferenceValidationDetails` generation on the transaction hot-path.

### 2. How do you know it is a real problem?
Profiling data collected from production mainnet nodes (`pprof.inferenced.samples.cpu.001.pb.gz`) identified stats queries/recording and signature validation as the primary time contributors. Specifically, on-chain stats operations account for ~40% of the execution time in `FinishInference` and ~30% in `StartInference`. The `proposals/inference-performance/README.md` and subsequent markdown documents thoroughly outline the tracing results confirming these bottlenecks.

### 3. How does this solve the problem?
This PR completely overhauls the hot-paths for inference recording via several targeted architectural shifts:
*   **Task II (Off-Chain Stats Migration):** Removes the heavy `SetDeveloperStats` writes from the on-chain `SetInference` hot-path. Instead, the `inference_finished` event is enriched with a full payload of details, allowing the Decentralized API Node to listen and store developer stats entirely off-chain (using Postgres or File storage).
*   **Task III (Deferred Processing):** Defers the expensive construction of `InferenceValidationDetails` and the incrementing of `EpochGroupData.NumberOfRequests` to the `EndBlocker`. It uses the fast, in-memory `transient_store` to enqueue completed inference IDs during the block to be processed in batch.
*   **Task IV (Participant & SPRT Optimization):** Consolidates duplicate `SetParticipant` and `SetInference` writes into a single final write per transaction. Optimizes the status math by precomputing expensive SPRT log-likelihood ratios (`Decimal.Ln`) when parameters change and caching them in the `transient_store`. Introduces a transactional `GetParams` context cache to prevent redundant state reads.
*   **Task VII (Rolling Windows):** Replaces the previous dynamic pricing and invalidation limit algorithms (which depended on the now-removed on-chain developer stats) with highly efficient `O(1)` on-chain rolling windows for model token loads and inference counts.

### 4. What risks does this introduce? How do we mitigate these risks?
*   **Risk:** The experimental file-based stats storage can cause inode exhaustion, performance degradation, and crashes on high-volume nodes.
    *   **Mitigation:** PostgreSQL is the strongly recommended and default production backend. File storage is explicitly flagged as experimental and is disabled by default (requires `DAPI_STATS_FILE_STORAGE_ENABLED=true`).
*   **Risk:** Off-chain API nodes dropping inference statuses if an inference state is invalidated or revalidated after completion.
    *   **Mitigation:** Introduced a new `inference_status_updated` event emitted on `MsgValidation`, `MsgInvalidateInference`, and `MsgRevalidateInference` so API nodes can keep their off-chain databases cleanly synchronized.
*   **Risk:** Failed transactions leaving orphaned data in the validation queue.
    *   **Mitigation:** The `transient_store` queue is strictly block-scoped. It automatically rolls back on failed transactions and is wiped clean at the end of the block, preventing state bloat.

### 5. How do you know this PR fixes the problem?
Extensive benchmarks and tests were added alongside these changes:
*   `BenchmarkComputeStatus` micro-benchmarks prove the mathematical optimizations drastically reduce CPU cycles. 
*   `TestEndBlock_ProcessesPendingInferenceValidationQueue` and related tests prove the deferred EndBlocker logic correctly batches and persists validation details. 
*   E2E API tests (`StatsApiE2ETest`) prove that off-chain stats collection and querying behave identically to the legacy on-chain logic. 
*(Note: Final production timing verification on a live node is still flagged as a pending TODO in the proposal docs, but the architectural removal of the identified bottlenecks guarantees a massive reduction in I/O and CPU).*

---

### Other Notes
*   **Transient Store Initialization:** This upgrade requires validators to initialize the new transient store keys (`TransientStoreKey`). The upgrade handlers (`upgrades_test.go`, etc.) have been updated to mount this store type properly.
*   **API Node Postgres Requirements:** The decentralized API node now expects a `PGHOST` environment variable to initialize the PostgreSQL off-chain stats backend. A `DisabledStorage` fallback is gracefully provided if not configured, which will disable the stats endpoints.
*   **New Stats Endpoints:** Several new endpoints (`/v1/stats/*`) were added to the public server to expose the newly off-chain developer stats for dashboards. The legacy `getGovernanceModelsLegacy` is retained temporarily for backwards compatibility.
*   **Managed Pruning:** The new off-chain storage automatically handles retention and pruning of old inference records. By default, it runs a background loop holding 30 days of data (`DAPI_STATS_RETENTION_DAYS`).

* Integration test fixes (#871)

# PR Description

### 1. What problem does this solve?
This PR addresses several distinct issues related to integration test reliability and local build environments:
* **Governance Tests Failing:** Governance tests were failing because `BandwidthLimitsParams` lacked a defined default/genesis value, causing state initialization errors.
* **Build Failures:** The `proxy-ssl` Docker image failed to build in emulated Linux/AMD64 environments due to Go toolchain mismatches.
* **Integration Test Flakiness:** Several integration tests (most notably `NodeDisableInferenceTests.kt`, `NodeAdminStateTests.kt`, `BandwidthLimiterTests.kt`, and `CollateralTests.kt`) suffered from race conditions, timing sensitivities, and transient network errors due to static sleep times and asynchronous ML node startup.

### 2. How do you know it is a real problem?
Simply put, the Integration Test Github Action had both consistent failures and intermittent failures that needed to be fixed. Without passing tests, functionality might break without us knowing and the speed of analysis on Integration Test runs is greatly slowed down.


### 3. How does this solve the problem?
* **Genesis Parameters:** Adds `BandwidthLimitsParams` with sensible default values to the `DefaultParams()` function in `inference-chain/x/inference/types/params.go`. This ensures the chain initializes correctly and governance tests can pass.
* **Test Resiliency Improvements:** 
  * Replaces static `Thread.sleep` calls with robust, deadline-based polling (e.g., waiting for RPC and ML node readiness via `waitForMlNodesToLoad`).
  * Introduces specific retry helpers like `waitForSuccessfulInference` and `collectSuccessfulInferenceResults` to handle transient network/routing failures elegantly.
  * Adjusts testing bounds, such as increasing retry attempts in `InvalidationTests.kt` and defining specific `downtimeHThreshold` parameters in `CollateralTests.kt`.
* **Logic Refinements:** 
  * Updates `filterNodesForValidation` in the PoC validator to strictly verify if a node `ShouldBeOperational` based on the current epoch and phase.
  * Simplifies `ShouldCheckPreserveNode` to evaluate based strictly on the inference's `StartBlockHeight` rather than the timeout block, resolving retroactive expiration edge cases.

### 4. What risks does this introduce? How do we mitigate these risks?
* **Risks:** 
* The PoC node filtering logic now relies heavily on the `epochState`. If the epoch state fails to load (`nil`), the PoC orchestrator will throw an error and fail to filter nodes.
* **Mitigations:** 
  * Explicit `nil` checks and error logging have been added to `validator.go` to handle missing epoch states gracefully. Furthermore, the heavily refactored test suite explicitly validates these node filtering and admin state boundaries.

### 5. How do you know this PR fixes the problem?
By observing the CI pipeline. We have now had 3 runs in a row with 0 failures.

---

### Other Notes
* **proxy-ssl** local build fix. This part of the build was failing intermittently for local builds.
* **Inference Payload Jitter Refinement:** The `retrievePayloadsWithRetry` function in the decentralized API was updated to use a tighter initial retry interval (`10 * time.Second` with a 10s jitter max) for the first attempt, followed by the standard `2 * time.Minute` interval for subsequent attempts. This optimizes payload retrieval latency for initial failures while avoiding spamming on prolonged outages.
* **Significant Test Rewrites:** Reviewers should pay special attention to `NodeDisableInferenceTests.kt` and `NodeAdminStateTests.kt`. These were substantially rewritten to accurately model node disablement behavior across epoch boundaries, including manually stopping API containers via Docker commands to accurately test reward claiming mechanics.

* Inference msgs optimization: optimize key verification (#779)

# Pull Request Description

### 1. What problem does this solve?
This PR addresses the computational overhead of performing redundant cryptographic signature verifications on the blockchain. Previously, the network performed multiple EC signature verifications across different inference lifecycle messages (`StartInference` and `FinishInference`) and verified an executor signature that is no longer strictly required by protocol policy.

### 2. How do you know it is a real problem?
Cryptographic signature verification is a known computationally expensive operation in blockchain environments. Reducing the number of signature recoveries directly decreases CPU load and block processing time. In profiling of the live production chain, verifying signature was more than 50% of the time spent during each Start or Finish Message processing, severely limiting our total throughput.

### 3. How does this solve the problem?
This PR implements an optimized **first-message verifies, second-message compares** policy for inference messages:
*   Whichever message arrives **first** (`StartInference` or `FinishInference`) pays the computational cost of cryptographic signature verification. 
*   The **second** message bypasses cryptographic verification and instead performs lightweight equality checks on its signature-bound fields against the persisted state of the first message.

**Specific Policy Implementations:**
*   **Dev Signature:** Verified on the first message. The second message only compares `original_prompt_hash`, `request_timestamp`, `transfer_agent`, and `requested_by`.
*   **Transfer Agent (TA) Signature:** Verified on the first message *only* if it is a finish message. If `StartInference` arrives first, TA verification is skipped and deferred to the `FinishInference` message.
*   **Executor Signature:** Cryptographic verification is completely disabled/removed by policy. All facts in the FinishInference message are already cryptographically signed by the Executor
*   **State Persistence:** Added persistence for `OriginalPromptHash`, `PromptHash`, and `RequestedBy` during message processing in `inference_state.go` so the second message has the necessary state to compare against.

### 4. What risks does this introduce? How do we mitigate these risks?
**Risks:**
By bypassing strict cryptographic signature checks on the second message, there is a risk of accepting spoofed or mutated data if the fields bounded by the original signature are not thoroughly validated against the already-verified state.

**Mitigations:**
*   **Strict Component Comparison:** Added comprehensive cross-message comparison methods (`compareDevComponents`, `compareStartTAComponents`, `compareFinishTAComponents`, `compareStartModelField`, etc.) that strictly validate the incoming message fields against the trusted state.
*   **Extensive Testing:** Added `msg_server_cross_message_comparison_test.go` which rigorously tests both in-order and out-of-order message flows, ensuring that component mismatches (e.g., mismatched prompt hashes, request timestamps, requested_by fields, or models) are actively rejected with newly added error types (`ErrDevComponentMismatch`, `ErrTAComponentMismatch`, `ErrInferenceRoleMismatch`).

### 5. How do you know this PR fixes the problem?
The diff clearly demonstrates the removal of the executor signature `VerifyKeys` call and wraps the remaining `VerifyKeys` calls for Dev and TA signatures in conditional blocks checking `existingInference.StartProcessed()` or `existingInference.FinishedProcessed()`. Because the code paths now short-circuit into O(1) string/integer comparisons instead of ecrecover operations for the second message, the computational overhead is guaranteed to be reduced. The passing test suites confirm that validation integrity is maintained despite the optimization. Local benchmarking has indicated significant perf improvement, testnet will be tested along with eventual benchmarks for mainnet.

---

## Other Notes

*   **Atomic Upgrade Required:** During the upgrade, we must guarantee that both the `decentralized-api` and the `inferenced` binary are upgraded simultaneously. Clients failing to upgrade will start failing to process inferences due to the new hashing and signature verification rules.
*   **State Marker Update:** The `calculations.startProcessed()` logic has been refactored into a method on the `Inference` type (`StartProcessed()`). It now checks `AssignedTo != ""` rather than checking the `PromptHash` (since `ProcessFinishInference` now populates `PromptHash`, it is no longer a reliable marker for start processing).
*   **Invariant Enforcement:** `FinishInference` now explicitly checks that the `Creator` matches the `ExecutedBy` address, throwing an `ErrInferenceRoleMismatch` if they differ. This is why we can trust that the data in `FinishInference` is cryptographically verified.
*   **Testermint (Kotlin Integration Tests) Updates:**
    *   Added `PromptHashing.kt` to centralize and strictly mirror the Go backend's canonical JSON serialization and prompt hashing behavior.
    *   Updated the `InferenceTests` to reflect the new policy: tests now expect a bad TA signature on a `StartInference` message to succeed (since it is deferred), and a bad EA signature on a `FinishInference` message to succeed (since it is disabled).
    *   Added a missing check in the public API handler (`post_chat_handler.go`) to ensure an explicitly empty `PromptHash` is rejected with a `400 Bad Request`.

* MsgValidation and MsgClaimRewards performance optimization (#874)

Improves performance of the inference message path, mainly around MsgValidate and MsgClaimRewards, by reducing hot-path state lookups and refactoring related validation and reward logic.

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **High Risk**
> Touches consensus-critical validation, reward-claiming, and on-chain storage layout, including a migration and new pruning state, so regressions could affect state correctness or liveness if edge cases are missed.
> 
> **Overview**
> **Performance-focused refactor of `MsgValidation` and reward claiming.** `MsgValidation` now relies on a per-block transient cache of epoch model/group metadata (threshold, total weight, participant weight/reputation) built in `BeginBlock`, avoiding repeated store reads; it also ignores validations that arrive more than one epoch after the inference epoch and writes inference updates without re-touching pruning indexes.
> 
> **Epoch group validations storage is reworked.** The legacy `EpochGroupValidations` aggregate map is replaced in hot paths by a new `EpochGroupValidationEntry` keyset keyed by `(epoch, participant, inferenceId)`, with a `v0.2.11` upgrade migration to backfill current/previous epoch entries and then clear the old map. A new pruner and `PruningState.epoch_group_validations_pruned_epoch` track/cleanup these entries, and the “list all epoch group validations” query/CLI is disabled.
> 
> **Claim-time validation is now configurable.** Adds `ValidationParams.claim_validation_enabled` and updates `MsgClaimRewards` to inject/load params and only run expensive `validateClaim` checks when the flag is enabled (tests updated accordingly).

* Fix the application.db bloat issue. (#867)

* fix: resolve pruning freeze caused by gaps in pruneSnapshotHeights

  When snapshot creation is missed (network failure, disk full, timeout,
  node restart, or fresh state-sync where [0]=0), gaps appear in the
  pruneSnapshotHeights array. The existing chain-trimming logic in
  HandleSnapshotHeight stops at the first gap, so pruneSnapshotHeights[0]
  never advances — permanently freezing pruning.

  GetPruningHeight uses pruneSnapshotHeights[0] to cap the maximum height
  IAVL will prune to: snHeight = [0] + snapshotInterval - 1. When [0] is
  stale, all versions above that cap accumulate indefinitely.

  Add a for loop (after sort, before chain trimming) that evicts stale
  leading heights where the gap to the next element exceeds
  snapshotInterval. Mid-array gaps are resolved progressively — each
  HandleSnapshotHeight call resolves the leading gap, then chain trimming
  runs until the next one.

  Covers: fresh state-sync ([0]=0), single missed snapshot, multiple
  consecutive gaps, and normal operation (no-op when no gaps).

### Summary of Fixes
- **GEB-03:** Duplicate Slot Indices Inflate Threshold Coverage — `checkThresholdAndAggregate()` no longer accepts duplicate slot indices in a single submission, preventing signers from tripping the threshold early.
- **GEB-04:** Incorrect Signing Threshold in checkThresholdAndAggregate() — The signing logic now uses the correct mathematical threshold `tSlotsDegree + 1` instead of hardcoding `totalSlots/2 + 1`.
- **GEB-12:** Dealer Commitments Not Bound to Threshold Degree — The system now enforces that the number of commitments matches the polynomial degree, preventing malicious dealers from manipulating the reconstruction threshold.
- **GEB-13:** Aggregation of BLS Partial Signature Does Not Eliminate Duplicates — Duplicate slot indices are now deduplicated during signature accumulation, ensuring only valid aggregate signatures are produced.
- **GEB-17:** Dealer Validation Majority Is Too Weak for Safe Key Recovery — Consensus for dealer validity now uses a strict majority against all participants, preventing unsafe key recovery majorities that could be caused by abstentions.
- **GEB-18:** Slot Donation Picks Under-Allocated Donor, Enabling Sybil Weight Inflation — The tie-breaking mechanism for slot donation was fixed to prefer participants with larger remainders instead of smaller remainders, mitigating Sybil weight inflation attacks.
- **GEB-19:** Secret Shares Logged in `logging.Debug` — Reconstructed secret key shares are no longer completely logged in `logging.Debug`, preventing leakage of sensitive private key material.
- **GEB-23:** Missing Validation of `MsgRequestThresholdSignature.ValidateBasic()` — Added validation to ensure `ChainId` and `RequestId` are exactly 32 bytes and that data chunks follow the correct sizes, preventing EVM-incompatible payloads.
- **GEB-24:** Insufficient Validation for Dealer Part Submissions in `MsgSubmitDealerPart.ValidateBasic()` — Enforces strict size bounds for compressed G2 commitments and limits per-participant encrypted shares to prevent malformed or oversized payloads during DKG.
- **GEB-25:** Missing Validation in Group Key Validation Signatures in `MsgSubmitGroupKeyValidationSignature.ValidateBasic()` — Added missing presence and length validations for signature fields and set boundary limits on the `SlotIndices` array.
- **GEB-26:** Missing Validation in Partial Signature Submissions in `MsgSubmitPartialSignature.ValidateBasic()` — Added validation on partial signature bytes and sets an upper bound on the list of slot indices to prevent state bloat and processing errors.
- **GEB-27:** Unbounded DealerValidity in Verification Vector Submissions of `MsgSubmitVerificationVector.ValidateBasic()` — Properly bounds and limits `DealerValidity` bitmaps from exceeding the maximum allowed limits to prevent memory and state inflation.

* IBC Trade Support (#814)

# Proposal: Enable IBC Token Trading via Governance

## Overview
This proposal enables the trading of **Native and IBC Tokens** in the Gonka Liquidity Pool and Community Sale contract by introducing a dedicated governance approval workflow. This is specifically designed to support stablecoin representations in the Cosmos ecosystem (such as `USDC` or `USDT`) as alternative to CW20 bridge wrapper.

## Core Design Principles
1.  **Strict Separation of Logic**: IBC-specific logic lives in separate files (`ibc_wrapped_token.go`) and uses separate messages (`MsgApproveIbcTokenForTrading`).
2.  **Unified Available List**: While logic is separate, approvals are stored in the *same* underlying map (`LiquidityPoolApprovedTokensMap`). This ensures the existing `ApprovedTokensForTrade` query automatically returns both CW20 and IBC tokens to UIs.
3.  **Distinct Validation Paths**: The Liquidity Pool Contract will use a *new* specific query (`ValidateIbcTokenForTrade`) for IBC tokens, acknowledging that their metadata lives in the Bank module, not the Bridge store.

## Changes

### 1. Chain Logic (`inference-chain`)

#### A. New Protocol Definitions
*   **Message**: `MsgApproveIbcTokenForTrading`
    *   **Usage**: Governance submits this to valid an IBC denom.
    *   **Convention**: `ChainId="ibc"`, `ContractAddress="ibc/HASH"` (or native denom).
*   **Query**: `ValidateIbcTokenForTrade`
    *   **Usage**: Called by the Liquidity Pool contract to verify an IBC token before adding it.

#### B. Implementation (`x/inference/keeper`)
We introduce new files to mirror the existing bridge functionality but adapted for IBC:
*   `ibc_wrapped_token.go`: Handles storage of approvals (allowing `/` in regex).
*   `query_ibc_token.go`: Handles validation logic.
    *   **Metadata Check**: Verifies `BankKeeper.GetDenomMetaData(ctx, denom)` exists (ensuring UI has decimals/symbol).
    *   **Allowlist Check**: Verifies the token is in `LiquidityPoolApprovedTokensMap`.
*   `msg_server_register_ibc_token_metadata.go`: Handles dual-writing of metadata.
    *   **Internal Map**: Stores metadata in `WrappedTokenMetadataMap` for bridge-specific logic.
    *   **Bank Module integration**: Calls `BankKeeper.SetDenomMetaData` to ensure the IBC token decimals and symbol are visible via standard Cosmos bank queries and explorers.

#### C. Contract Migration Integration (`app/upgrades/v0_2_11`)
*   **Upgrade Handler**: Added CosmWasm contract migration steps directly into the consensus upgrade.
*   **Execution**: Parses the governance `Plan.Info` field for a JSON string containing `community_sale_address` and `new_code_id`. Utilizing `wasmkeeper`, it safely executes the contract migration, automatically triggering the update payload to enable new configuration `allow_all_trade_tokens`.

### 2. Contract Logic (`liquidity-pool`)

#### A. Dynamic Token Validation
*   **Method**: `validate_wrapped_token_for_trade` and `validate_ibc_token_for_trade`
*   **Logic Change**: The concept of a maintained `PAYMENT_TOKENS` map has been removed. The contract will now dynamically detect the token type and validate it against the chain module at the moment of purchase:
    *   **CW20**: Calls legacy `ValidateWrappedTokenForTrade`.
    *   **IBC/Native**: Calls new `ValidateIbcTokenForTrade`.
*   **Result**: Validates the token and retrieves necessary decimals directly from the chain API as the source of truth, removing duplicate state handling in the contract.

#### B. Native Token Purchase & Fund Accumulation
*   **Target**: `ExecuteMsg::PurchaseWithNative` and CW20 `Receive`
*   **Logic**:
    *   Accepts `info.funds` (strictly 1 coin).
    *   Dynamically validates the token and normalizes its USD value using the decimals sourced from the chain.
    *   **Accumulation**: Instead of forwarding received tokens to the `admin` account immediately, both CW20 and Native/IBC tokens safely accumulate in the contract's balance. This design change was necessary because the governance (admin) account does not have the ability to sign and execute CW20 smart contract transfer messages. If forwarded directly to governance, CW20 tokens would become permanently stuck. Administrators can withdraw funds using dedicated withdrawal functions.

#### C. Direct Token Querying
*   **Target**: `QueryMsg::TestApprovedTokens` (superseding local state queries)
*   **Logic**:
    *   Directly queries the Chain API `ApprovedTokensForTrade` (which automatically returns *both* lists due to the shared state in `LiquidityPoolApprovedTokensMap`).
    *   Returns the unmerged raw list directly to the UI for consumption alongside dynamic rate querying.

### 3. Contract Logic (`community-sale`)

#### A. IBC Payment Support
*   **Method**: `ExecuteMsg::PurchaseWithNative`
*   **Logic**: Added support for purchasing GNK using native IBC tokens (e.g., Nobel USDC). The contract validates the IBC denom against the governance-maintained allowlist on the chain.

#### B. Dynamic Multi-Token Support (`allow_all_trade_tokens`)
*   **Method**: `ExecuteMsg::UpdateAllowAllTradeTokens` & `Config` structural changes.
*   **Logic**: Introduced an `allow_all_trade_tokens` configuration boolean. When enabled, the contract bypasses checks for a *single* strictly defined `accepted_ibc_denom` or `accepted_eth_contract`. Instead, it mirrors the `liquidity-pool` behavior by independently validating *any* received token dynamically against the on-chain governance allowlist (via `ValidateWrappedTokenForTrade` and `ValidateIbcTokenForTrade` queries).

#### C. Migration Support
*   **Migration Path**: Implemented a comprehensive `migrate` entry point that allows existing contract instances (like the one funded in Proposal 14) to be safely upgraded to this new version.
*   **Config Backwards-Compatibility**: If no V3 configuration updates are passed during migration, the contract perfectly retains the pre-migration restrictions (falling back to requiring the explicitly defined single token configurations) preserving the expected behavior of any active legacy contracts.

## Bridge Audit Fixes Included in This PR

The following security findings from Certik bridge audit have been resolved as part of this change set:

| ID | Severity | Title | Fixed In |
|----|----------|-------|----------|
| GEB-05 | Medium | Native Denom Auto-Detection Can Be Misconfigured in `community-sale` Contract | `community-sale/src/contract.rs` |
| GEB-07 | Minor | Weak Address Validation in `withdraw()` in `wrapped-token` Contract | `contracts/wrapped-token/src/contract.rs` |
| GEB-10 | Minor | Migration of `community‑sale` Lacks Compatibility Checks and State Validation | `community-sale/src/contract.rs` |
| GEB-11 | Info | `InstantiateMsg.marketing` Is Ignored | `contracts/wrapped-token/src/contract.rs` |
| GEB-15 | Medium | Cross-Chain Address Collision in `IsBridgeContractAddress()` | `keeper/bridge_native.go` |
| GEB-36 | Medium | Authority Mismatch In `MigrateAllWrappedTokenContracts()` | `keeper/bridge_wrapped_token.go` |
| GEB-39 | Minor | Missing Validation of `msg.Amount` Being Positive in `MsgRequestBridgeWithdrawal` | `types/message_request_bridge_withdrawal.go` |

### Summary of Fixes

- **GEB-05**: Native denom is now passed explicitly as an instantiation parameter instead of being auto-detected from bank supply, preventing manipulation attacks.
- **GEB-07**: `withdraw()` now validates that `destination_address` conforms to the Ethereum address format (0x + 40 hex chars) to prevent permanent fund loss from invalid destinations.
- **GEB-10**: The community-sale migrate() entry point only reads the previous contract version and writes a new version. It does not validate the previous contract name/version nor confirm that required state (CONFIG) exists. A mistaken migration from an unrelated contract or a corrupted state will still “succeed,” but subsequent calls that load CONFIG will error, leaving the contract unusable.
- **GEB-11**: Removed the unused `marketing` field from `InstantiateMsg` — marketing info is hardcoded and governed on-chain via the governance module.
- **GEB-15**: `IsBridgeContractAddress()` now takes `chainId` as a parameter and checks the exact `(chainId, address)` pair, preventing cross-chain address collisions with CREATE2/deterministic deployments.
- **GEB-36**: `MigrateAllWrappedTokenContracts()` now correctly uses the governance address (matching how contracts were instantiated) instead of the inference module address, resolving the authorization mismatch that caused DoS.
- **GEB-39**: `MsgRequestBridgeWithdrawal.ValidateBasic()` now parses and validates that `Amount` is a positive integer using `math.NewIntFromString`, rejecting zero, negative, and non-numeric values.

* Fix TestRegularPocScenario and Collateral tests (#881)

## Stabilize `TestRegularPocScenario` by synchronizing with async reconciliation

**Summary**

This PR fixes flakiness in `TestRegularPocScenario` in the decentralized-api event listener tests.

The test was assuming broker reconciliation had already completed while the system under test still performs node transitions asynchronously. Under local reruns and in PRs, that timing gap caused the test to observe in-flight work and fail nondeterministically.

**What changed**

- Added a thread-safe getter for `InitGenerateV2Called` on the ML node mock.
- Updated `TestRegularPocScenario` to:
  - wait for both nodes to actually reach `INFERENCE` after the first reconciliation window,
  - assert the expected inference-side calls at that synchronized point,
  - continue checking that `InitGenerateV2` does not happen early, but do so with a mutex-safe read,
  - transition to the next epoch only after the initial inference reconciliation has completed.

**Why this is justified**

The previous test was not only checking behavior, it was also implicitly asserting a scheduling detail: that no background reconciliation goroutine was touching the mock at the instant the assertion ran. That is not part of the product contract.

The real intent of the test is:

- PoC generation should not start before the epoch boundary.
- Nodes should enter inference during the inference phase.
- At the epoch transition, nodes should move into PoC generation and later back to inference.

Those assertions are still preserved. The test is now aligned with the system’s actual async execution model instead of racing it.

**Why this does not hide a real issue**

This change does not relax any product behavior expectations.

It does **not** allow early PoC generation.
It does **not** mask incorrect state transitions.
It does **not** change production code paths or broker behavior.

What it removes is a false failure mode caused by reading mock state while legitimate async reconciliation was in progress. In failing runs, the product still reached the correct PoC state; the test was flaking because it sampled counters at an unstable moment, and in some runs it also advanced the epoch before the initial `InferenceUp` reconciliation had finished.

In other words, the failure was caused by the test outrunning the system, not by the system violating the intended behavior.

**Evidence**

Before this change, the test reproduced locally with a high failure rate across repeated `go test -count=1` runs.
After synchronizing the test with the async reconciliation lifecycle, the same repeated local reruns passed consistently.

**Risk**

Low.

- Production code behavior is unchanged.
- The only non-test addition is a mutex-safe accessor on the mock client.
- The test now verifies the same behavioral contract at stable synchronization points.

* # Inference shards / subnets (#877)

# Inference shards

Per-inference transactions on mainnet limit throughput to ~277 inferences/sec. Requests could be batched, but computation and state growth per request make this unscalable to hundreds of thousands of inferences.

This PR moves all per-inference communication off-chain. The chain processes only two transactions per session: one to put coins in escrow and assign a subgroup of hosts, one to settle at the end. All inference requests and validations happen inside the subgroup directly, over a long session.

To close the session, the user submits the final usage state signed by a supermajority of hosts (threshold: 2/3 slot-weighted). Both sides have a clear incentive to settle -- the user recovers unused escrow balance, the subgroup gets paid from it.

As each subgroup achieves consensus for the final state, the architecture becomes:
- main blockchain
- many sub-chains / shards with extremely lightweight architecture

```
+-----------+     +-------------------+     +----------------------------+
|   User    |     |      Mainnet      |     |  Subnet (one per session)  |
+-----------+     +-------------------+     +----------------------------+
      |                    |                             |
      | 1. MsgCreateEscrow |                             |
      |    (100GNK)        |                             |
      | -----------------> |                             |
      | <- escrow_id,      |                             |
      |    group=[h1..hN]  |                             |
      |                    |                             |
      | 2. POST /chat (req1) --------------------------> |
      | 3. POST /chat (req2) --------------------------> |
      | 4. POST /chat (reqN) --------------------------> |
      |    ...             |                             |
      |                    |                             |
      | 5. MsgSettleEscrow |                             |
      |   (finalState,     |                             |
      |    signatures, ..) |                             |
      | -----------------> |                             |
      | <- user refund +   |                             |
      |    hosts paid      |                             |
+-----------+     +-------------------+     +----------------------------+
```

The user acts as the sequencer. They order transactions, propagate state to hosts, and initiate any timeout or settlement procedures. The user is directly incentivized to do this -- unresolved inferences lock escrow balance, and unrecorded host work reduces the user's refund at settlement.

## Implementation

This is version 0 of subnets. The protocol will evolve, but it is important to start testing on a real chain now. Early mainnet testing will surface issues that unit tests and local testnets cannot.

We created a separate standalone module `/subnet/` with no Cosmos SDK dependencies. This boundary exists so the subnet code can be tested and reasoned about independently. Long-term goal is to fully separate the subnet service from `decentralized-api` into its own process.

In this early version, the subnet module is compiled into `decentralized-api` and runs in the same process. This avoids the operational overhead of a separate deployment while the protocol stabilizes.

### What lives where

`/subnet/` contains the core protocol logic: state machine that applies diffs, tracks inference lifecycle through 7 states (pending, started, finished, challenged, validated, invalidated, timed_out), computes costs, validates secp256k1 signatures (user, executor, host-proposed), and derives executor slots deterministically from nonce (`inference_id % group_size`). Includes HTTP transport via Echo, gossip with K-fanout nonce propagation, and SQLite storage (WAL mode) for append-only diff logs. Uses `go-ethereum/crypto` for signing, `google.golang.org/protobuf` for deterministic serialization. No shared types with mainnet protos.

The subnet defines 8 off-chain transaction types: `MsgStartInference`, `MsgConfirmStart`, `MsgFinishInference`, `MsgTimeoutInference`, `MsgValidation`, `MsgValidationVote`, `MsgRevealSeed`, `MsgFinalizeRound`.

`decentralized-api/internal/subnet/` adapts the subnet module to dapi. `EngineAdapter` wraps dapi's broker and completionapi to execute inferences on vLLM nodes. `ValidationAdapter` re-executes inferences with enforced tokens and compares logits. `ChainBridge` implements `MainnetBridge` via gRPC for escrow queries and warm key verification. `HostManager` creates sessions lazily per escrow, recovers them from SQLite on restart, and registers endpoints on `/subnet/v1/`.

`inference-chain/x/inference/keeper/` adds two new chain messages. `MsgCreateSubnetEscrow` locks funds and samples 16 slots via weighted random sampling using `GetSlotsFromSorted` (same algorithm as PoC validation). `MsgSettleSubnetEscrow` verifies the settlement proof -- recomputes `host_stats_hash`, checks `SHA256(host_stats_hash || rest_hash || phase_byte) == state_root`, verifies 2/3+1 slot-weighted secp256k1 signatures, distributes payments to validators proportionally to accumulated cost, refunds the remainder. Unsettled escrows older than 2 epochs are pruned; funds split equally among unique validators.

### Protocol properties

Escrow accounting is checked per-inference. `MsgStartInference` reserves `(input_length + max_tokens) * token_price` from the escrow balance. `MsgFinishInference` computes actual cost from real token counts and releases the surplus. `MsgTimeoutInference` releases the full reservation.

Hosts measure executor performance. Each slot accumulates missed count (timeouts), invalid count (failed validations), and total cost in `host_stats`. These stats are part of the settlement proof.

Validation is probabilistic. Each host derives a deterministic seed by signing the escrow ID bytes and taking the first 8 bytes. `ShouldValidate` computes a per-inference probability from the seed and a configurable rate. Seeds are revealed during a mandatory finalizing round before settlement. Compliance (required vs completed validations) is computed deterministically from revealed seeds.

Warm keys are supported. Hosts can sign with operator keys authorized via on-chain authz grants. The first diff-contained signature from a slot binds the warm key permanently for the session. Warm key bindings are part of the state root.

Timeout verification requires signed votes from other hosts as evidence. Two reasons: `refused` (executor never signed receipt, pending -> timed_out) and `execution` (executor signed but didn't finish within deadline, started -> timed_out). Threshold: total_slots/2.

Equivocation (user submitting conflicting diffs at the same nonce) is detectable via gossip and results in session termination.

State root is `SHA256(host_stats_hash || rest_hash || phase_byte)`, where `rest_hash = SHA256(balance || inferences_hash || warm_keys_hash)`. All hashing uses deterministic proto serialization.

## Next steps

This PR proposes merging subnets into v0.2.11 with limited access. `AllowedCreatorAddresses` in `SubnetEscrowParams` restricts who can create escrows. The plan is to test on mainnet, resolve issues, and remove the restriction in v0.2.12-v0.2.13.

The subnet module is experimental. All parameters are conservative defaults designed to avoid harm to the chain: group size 16 (slots per escrow), escrow bounds 5-10 GNK, max 100 escrows per epoch, unsettled escrows pruned after 2 epochs.

Issues to resolve before wider rollout:

- Aggregating subnet stats on mainnet. Subnet `host_stats` (missed, invalid, cost, validations) do not yet feed into the bitcoin-style block reward or PoC reputation system.
- Skeptical review of the state machine. Adversarial edge cases (e.g. user-host collusion, strategic timeout abuse) need formal analysis.
- Timeout flows end-to-end. Refusal and execution timeout verification need integration testing under real network conditions.
- Pricing. Token price is flat per session, we need to adapt dynamic
- BLS signature aggregation. `MsgSettleSubnetEscrow` currently carries N individual secp256k1 signatures. Aggregating into a single BLS signature reduces transaction size.
- ...

fix: ensure /data/collections dir exists with correct ownership for collections DB (#728)

The solr-search container failed to start with sqlite3.OperationalError because
the /data/collections directory (mounted as a named Docker volume) was created
as root-owned. The app user (uid 1000) could not write the SQLite database file.

Changes:
- Dockerfile: add /data/collections to mkdir so it exists at build time and
  gets chowned to app:app
- entrypoint.sh: chown /data/collections at runtime (handles pre-existing
  root-owned volumes or bind mounts)

commit.message:
Implement headless video recording tool and improve performance (#182)

* Add record.py: headless video recording tool for gameplay capture

Implements all core components of the recording system:
- CLI with argparse: model_path, scenario, --output-path, --count,
  --only-wins, --show-attention, --no-observation, --no-model-actions,
  --device, --fps
- VideoRecorder: OpenCV MP4 writer with temp-file strategy for
  --only-wins filtering and sequential triforce_NN.mp4 naming
- FrameComposer: 1920x1080 canvas with scaled gameplay on left,
  side panel on right (observation + action panels)
- ObservationRenderer: Pillow-based entity list, directions, booleans
  with FiraCode Nerd Font and fallback
- ActionRenderer: animated GIF sprites in 2x2 grid with directional
  probabilities, SWORD/BEAMS combination, <0.1% threshold hiding
- AttentionOverlay: JET colormap heatmap ported from game_view.py
  with bilinear upsample and 55% alpha blend
- Main recording loop with --only-wins success detection and
  --max-attempts safety limit
- GIF sprite assets copied to img/
- record.py added to pylint targets (passes at 10.00/10)

* Fix record.py: single forward pass, CUDA device support, end-to-end verified

- Eliminate duplicate model forward pass: combined get_action_and_probabilities()
  does action selection and probability computation in one inference call (~2x speedup)
- Add device transfer for obs/mask tensors so --device cuda works correctly
- Fix attention weights to move obs to model device before inference
- Remove _to_device helper by inlining into get_action_and_probabilities
- Trim module to stay under pylint 1000-line limit (997 lines)
- End-to-end verified: 1920x1080 MP4 with gameplay, observation panel, action panel,
  and attention overlay all producing correct output

* Fix video codec: replace OpenCV mp4v with ffmpeg H.264

OpenCV's mp4v (MPEG-4 Part 2) codec is not supported by many players
including VLC. Replace the OpenCV VideoWriter with an ffmpeg subprocess
pipe using libx264 for proper H.264 encoding. Also replace the one
remaining cv2.resize call with Pillow to drop the OpenCV dependency
entirely from record.py.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* Fix observation panel: reorder sections, fix arrows and icons

* Revert "Fix video codec: replace OpenCV mp4v with ffmpeg H.264"

This reverts commit 53df806a5a5b27f88fe8e79f8af7ff09dabe7bf7.

* Fix end_reason: read from rewards.ending instead of state info

The end reason was always empty because it was read from
state_change.state.info['end_reason'] which doesn't exist.
The ScenarioWrapper puts it on rewards.ending instead.

commit.message:
fix: address Copilot review on auth cookie persistence

Issue 1 — Cookie refresh defeats remember_me:
  The validate endpoint now reads the 'rmb' claim from the JWT and only
  sets max_age for persistent sessions (remember_me=true).  Session
  cookies are refreshed without max_age so they expire when the browser
  closes.

Issue 2 — remember_me not sent from UI:
  Added TODO comment in LoginPage noting a 'Remember me' checkbox can be
  added later.  Default (false → session cookies) is the safe behavior.

Issue 3 — Token not set on cookie recovery:
  Changed isAuthenticated to Boolean(user) instead of
  Boolean(token && user) so cookie-only sessions are recognised.

Issue 4 — Missing cookie recovery test:
  Added test that verifies validate returning authenticated:true without
  any stored token results in an authenticated user.

Backend:
  - Added remember_me param to create_access_token (stored as 'rmb' JWT
    claim).
  - Added get_token_remember_me() helper.
  - set_auth_cookie now accepts max_age=None for session cookies.
  - Validate endpoint uses rmb claim to decide cookie max_age.

Frontend:
  - AuthContext always calls /v1/auth/validate on mount (cookie recovery).
  - isAuthenticated = Boolean(user) (works for cookie-only sessions).
  - Updated all affected tests; added cookie recovery test.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

fix: remove chunk exclusion from kNN params to preserve semantic search

The EXCLUDE_CHUNKS_FQ filter was incorrectly applied to build_knn_params(),
which excluded all chunk documents from vector search. Since embedding
vectors live on chunk docs (they carry parent_id_s), this would have made
semantic/hybrid search return zero kNN results.

- Keep chunk exclusion on build_solr_params() (keyword leg)
- Remove chunk exclusion from build_knn_params() (kNN leg)

commit.message:
perf: pre-build PCurves for planar face edges at shape load time (#145)

* perf: pre-build PCurves for planar face edges at shape load time

Callgrind insight 2: BRepClass3d_SolidClassifier::Perform() (called
on every Inside() query) invoked BRep_Tool::CurveOnPlane() per edge
per call. When no PCurve was stored in the BRep, OCCT computed it via
GeomProjLib::ProjectOnPlane, accounting for ~3.3% of all instructions.

Add a loop in ComputeBounds() that calls BRepLib::BuildPCurveForEdgeOnPlane()
for every edge on every face. (OCCT 7.9 exposes the per-edge overload;
BuildPCurveForEdgesOnPlane is a template taking an OCCT container, so the
explicit loop is cleaner.) This one-time O(N_edges) pass stores PCurves
into the BRep topology; subsequent CurveOnPlane() calls return the stored
result immediately, bypassing the GeomProjLib projection path.

commit.message:
fix: normalise internal-hostname document URLs for PDF viewer iframe

When the backend generates document URLs via request.url_for() behind a
reverse proxy, the resulting URL may contain an internal Docker hostname
(e.g. http://solr-search:8080/documents/...) or wrong scheme. The iframe
cannot reach these URLs from the browser.

resolveDocumentUrl() now detects absolute URLs whose path starts with
/documents/ and strips the origin, converting them to relative paths that
route correctly through the same-origin reverse proxy.

Closes #647

commit.message:
fix: persist auth via cookie and fix admin login loop

Bug #645 — Login does not persist:
- Add remember_me field to LoginRequest; when false the auth cookie
  becomes a session cookie (no max_age) that expires when the browser
  closes; when true, the cookie is persistent with the full JWT TTL.
- set_auth_cookie now accepts max_age=None for session cookies.

Bug #678 — Admin tab infinite login loop:
- The validate endpoint now refreshes the auth cookie on every
  successful validation. This ensures the cookie stays fresh as long
  as the JWT is valid, allowing nginx auth_request subrequests (used
  by /admin/streamlit/, /admin/solr/, etc.) to succeed.
- Previously, the cookie was only set at login time and could expire
  independently, causing nginx to reject requests to admin tabs and
  redirect to /?redirect=... in an infinite loop.

Frontend changes:
- apiFetch now uses credentials:'include' so cookies are sent even in
  cross-origin dev environments.
- AuthContext tries cookie-based session recovery when localStorage is
  empty (new tab, cleared data) by always calling /v1/auth/validate on
  mount instead of only when a stored token exists.

Closes #645
Closes #678

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Update Program.cs

Co-Authored-By: Copilot <198982749+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

fix: exclude chunk documents from search results

Books were appearing duplicated in the library and search results because
each text chunk indexed for a book was returned as a separate result.
The root cause: search queries had no filter to exclude chunk-level
documents (which have parent_id_s set) from results.

Added EXCLUDE_CHUNKS_FQ filter ('-parent_id_s:[* TO *]') to both
build_solr_params() and build_knn_params() in search_service.py.
This ensures only book-level (parent) documents appear in search
results, library browse, and facet queries.

Closes #648
